vulnerabilities.title: PermissionsRegistry.removeRole Corrupts _addressToRoles Using Wrong Source Array for Swap-and-Pop
vulnerabilities.summary: In removeRole(), when cleaning up _addressToRoles for affected users, the code uses `_roles[_roles.length - 1]` (the last element of the GLOBAL roles array) instead of `_addressToRoles[rta[i]][_addressToRoles[rta[i]].length - 1]` (the last element of the user's role array) for the swap-and-pop operation. This corrupts users' role lists with unrelated role entries.

description.desc: _addressToRoles[rta[i]][k] = _roles[_roles.length -1] uses _roles (global) instead of _addressToRoles[rta[i]]

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The hasRole mapping is correctly cleared, so runtime permission checks (VoterAdmin, Governance modifiers) are unaffected. However: (1) addressToRole() view function returns corrupted data, misleading off-chain systems and UIs. (2) Subsequent removeRoleFrom() on the affected address may fail to find roles that should exist (lost entries) or may try to remove phantom entries that aren't in hasRole, causing inconsistent state. (3) The _roleToAddresses mapping for the removed role is NOT cleaned up (only hasRole is set to false), leaving stale address entries. This is a definite code defect in an admin function.

vulnerabilities.title: SMNFT_BONUS Change Breaks totalSupply vs Individual Balance Invariant
vulnerabilities.summary: When team calls setSmNFTBonus, the global point's smNFTBonus is recalculated using calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) with the NEW rate at every _checkpoint, but individual smNFT token checkpoints retain their OLD stored smNFTBonus values until individually re-checkpointed. This breaks the invariant that totalSupply == sum(balanceOfNFT) and causes incorrect reward distributions.

description.desc: last_point.smNFTBonus = calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) uses current SMNFT_BONUS; individual checkpoints store fixed values

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. After any SMNFT_BONUS change: (1) totalSupply diverges from sum of individual balances, violating INV-STATE-005; (2) RewardsDistributor reward calculations use the divergent totalSupplyAtT, causing incorrect reward distribution for ALL users; (3) If bonus increases, totalSupply is inflated → all users receive less rebase rewards than deserved, with excess stuck in the contract; (4) If bonus decreases, totalSupply is deflated → reward distribution may exceed available tokens for later claimers; (5) Governance quorum calculations via getPastTotalSupply are incorrect.

vulnerabilities.title: VoterV3 voted Flag Stuck True When All Voted Gauges Are Killed, Blocking Token Transfers
vulnerabilities.summary: When poke() or vote() is called and all previously voted gauges are killed, _vote() calls _reset() (clearing weights) but only calls IVotingEscrow(_ve).voting(_tokenId) when _usedWeight > 0. Since all gauges are dead, _usedWeight = 0 and voting() is not called. However, the voted flag remains true from the previous vote, preventing transfers and withdrawals until the user calls reset() in the next epoch.

description.desc: if (_usedWeight > 0) IVotingEscrow(_ve).voting(_tokenId); — voted flag not cleared when _usedWeight == 0

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The user's veNFT is temporarily locked (cannot transfer or withdraw) for up to one epoch duration (1800 seconds / 30 minutes with current WEEK setting). This is a denial of service that blocks legitimate token operations. While recovery is possible in the next epoch via reset(), the user may not understand why their token is stuck. In a production setting with longer WEEK values, the impact could be more significant. Additionally, this can be weaponized by a governance actor who kills gauges to temporarily lock specific users' veNFTs.

vulnerabilities.title: AutoVotingEscrow.lockOwner Returns Incorrect Owner for Non-Existent or Released Token IDs
vulnerabilities.summary: lockOwner() uses tokenIdIndex mapping which defaults to 0 for non-existent tokenIds. If locks array is non-empty, lockOwner returns locks[0].owner for any tokenId not in the AVM, including released tokens whose index was deleted (reset to 0). The require check `index < locks.length` passes when locks is non-empty.

description.desc: tokenIdIndex defaults to 0; no validation that locks[index].tokenId == _tokenId

vulnerabilities.impact: CONFIRMED as a code defect. The lockOwner function violates its implied contract - it claims to return the owner of a given tokenId but can return the owner of a completely different token. Current impact is limited because existing callers use tokenIdToAVMId as a pre-check. However: (1) the function is part of the IAutoVotingEscrow interface and is callable by anyone, (2) future integrations may rely on it without the tokenIdToAVMId guard, (3) the defect could cause incorrect owner resolution in off-chain monitoring/UIs that query lockOwner directly.

vulnerabilities.title: Gas-bounded checkpoint logic may permanently desync voting power after ~5.3 days inactivity (WEEK=1800), breaking totalSupply/vote accounting
vulnerabilities.summary: VotingEscrow._checkpoint() advances global point history in a loop capped to 255 iterations, each step advancing by WEEK. With WEEK=1800 seconds, a single call can only catch up ~5.3 days. If the system goes longer without successful checkpointing, global history cannot be fully updated in one call, potentially causing persistent mis-accounting in totalSupplyAtT/totalSupplyAt and downstream reward distribution calculations.

description.desc: _checkpoint() for-loop `for (uint i = 0; i < 255; ++i)` advancing `t_i += WEEK` where WEEK = 1800 (BlackTimeLibrary)
Affected function: totalSupplyAtT(uint t, uint epoch, mapping(uint => int128) storage slope_changes, Data storage VotingBalanceLogicData)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH SEVERITY: (1) Global point_history desyncs from actual time, corrupting the core accounting invariant. (2) totalSupply() and totalSupplyAtT() return incorrect values, affecting RewardsDistributor claim calculations—users receive wrong reward amounts. (3) Governance voting via getPastTotalSupply returns wrong quorum values. (4) The issue compounds: each gap requires multiple manual checkpoint() calls to resolve, with no automatic mechanism. (5) With WEEK=1800, this can occur after just ~5.31 days of inactivity—a plausible scenario for any protocol.

vulnerabilities.title: Delegation Checkpoint Wipe-Out via Single Token Transfer
vulnerabilities.summary: In VotingDelegationLib.moveTokenDelegates, the source delegate filtering uses `ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep` to decide which tokens to keep. When a user who has delegated to address C transfers a single token, ALL tokens delegated to C by ANY user whose tokens are not owned by C are wiped from C's checkpoint. An attacker can wipe a major delegate's governance voting power by creating a minimal lock, delegating to the target, then transferring that token away.

description.desc: ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep used to filter tokens to keep; should be tId != _tokenId. When srcRep is a delegate (not the owner), no owned tokens match, wiping all entries.
Affected function: _transferFrom

vulnerabilities.impact:
CRITICAL. Any delegate's voting power can be reduced to zero (or to only self-owned tokens) in a single transaction costing minimal gas. This affects:
- Governance votes via getVotes/getPastVotes which sum balanceOfNFT for checkpoint tokenIds
- Any protocol decisions gated by delegation-based voting power
- The attack is repeatable: even if tokens are re-delegated, the attacker can wipe them again
- No economic cost to the attacker beyond gas

vulnerabilities.title: RewardsDistributor Reverts on Burned Tokens Locking Rebases
vulnerabilities.summary: Users who withdraw, merge, or split their veNFTs before claiming rebases will permanently lose them because `claim` attempts to transfer to `ownerOf`, which reverts for burned tokens.

description.desc: Transfer to ownerOf without checking for address(0)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact — any user who performs withdraw, merge, or split without first claiming rebases permanently loses those rebases. The tokens are stuck in the RewardsDistributor. With WEEK=1800 (30 minutes), rebases accumulate rapidly. Users who split/merge active locks are especially affected since they may have weeks of unclaimed rewards. The defect also means including a burned tokenId in claim_many() reverts the entire batch, potentially griefing other claims.

vulnerabilities.title: Bribes Fee-on-Transfer Insolvency
vulnerabilities.summary: If a fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted as a bribe, `notifyRewardAmount` records the pre-fee amount, causing the contract to become insolvent and late claimers to lose their rewards.

description.desc: Records full reward amount without checking received delta
Affected function: whitelistConnector

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is that all bribe rewards denominated in the FoT token will have a cumulative shortfall equal to the sum of all transfer fees. Late claimers will be unable to claim their entitled rewards. The severity depends on: (1) whether governance whitelists a FoT token, (2) the transfer fee percentage, and (3) the total volume of bribes in that token. Even small fees compound over multiple deposits, making insolvency increasingly likely over time. The code defect exists regardless of current configuration - it's a missing safeguard that should be present to handle a known token behavior pattern.

vulnerabilities.title: AVM-Delegated veNFT Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked in AutoVotingEscrow Contracts
vulnerabilities.summary: Bribe.getReward() sends rewards to ownerOf(tokenId) which returns the AutoVotingEscrow (AVE) instance address for AVM-managed tokens. The check `_owner == avm` compares the AVE address against the AutoVotingEscrowManager address — always false. Rewards are transferred to the AVE contract which has zero ERC20 recovery functions. Additionally, setOriginalOwner in the manager is a no-op (empty body), and VotingEscrow._transferFrom's `_to == avm` check never triggers because the actual transfer dest

description.desc: ownerOf returns AVE address; `_owner == avm` compares AVE vs Manager (always false); safeTransfer sends to AVE with no recovery
Affected function: setOriginalOwner
Affected function: _transferFrom

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL - Every AVM-managed veNFT permanently loses all bribe rewards. This affects all users who enable auto-voting. The total value at risk equals all bribe rewards allocated to AVM-managed tokens across all epochs. There is no recovery mechanism: the AVE contract has no ERC20 withdrawal function, and the tokens cannot be retrieved without a contract upgrade. Additionally, the RewardsDistributor.claim_many function has a similar issue for expired locks - it sends directly to ownerOf without AVM resolution.

vulnerabilities.title: Bribe Reward Claims Permanently DoS'd by Unbounded earned() Loop with WEEK=1800
vulnerabilities.summary: The Bribe.earned() function iterates over every epoch since lastEarn with no iteration cap. With WEEK=1800 seconds (30 minutes), epochs accumulate at 48/day. After ~8-10 real-world days without claiming, earned() requires 384-480 iterations, each performing two binary searches over storage (getPriorBalanceIndex + getPriorSupplyIndex). At ~39,000 gas per iteration, this exceeds Avalanche's 15M block gas limit. Since lastEarn is only updated inside getReward() which calls earned(), the DoS is self

description.desc: for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++) loop with no upper bound; numEpochs = (epochStart(now) - lastEarnEpoch) / WEEK; WEEK=1800
Affected function: epochStart

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL severity. Any user inactive for ~9 days permanently loses all accumulated and future bribe rewards for that token. This affects both manual voters and potentially AVM-managed tokens (though AVM has separate issues). Given that the protocol targets Avalanche with a 15M block gas limit, and WEEK=1800 means 48 epochs/day, the window for safe claiming is unreasonably short. Users who hold veNFTs as long-term investments (which is the intended use case for vote-escrowed tokens) are especially vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.title: RewardsDistributor claim_many Missing AVM Owner Resolution Causes Rebase Loss
vulnerabilities.summary: RewardsDistributor.claim_many() does not resolve AVM original owners for expired locks. For expired non-permanent locks, it sends rewards directly to ownerOf(tokenId) which is the AutoVotingEscrow contract. The single claim() function correctly checks `avm.tokenIdToAVMId(_tokenId) != 0` and resolves via getOriginalOwner(), but claim_many completely omits this logic. Rebase rewards for AVM-managed expired locks are sent to AVE contracts with no recovery.

description.desc: claim_many: `address _nftOwner = IVotingEscrow(_voting_escrow).ownerOf(_tokenId)` without AVM check; contrast with claim() lines 130-140 which checks avm.tokenIdToAVMId

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Any AVM-managed veNFT with a non-permanent, non-smNFT lock that expires while in the AVM will have its rebase rewards permanently lost if claim_many is called instead of claim. Since claim_many is permissionless and commonly used for batch operations, this is highly likely to occur. The AutoVotingEscrow contract has no token recovery mechanism, and no admin function exists to redirect the lost tokens. A contract upgrade would be needed to recover funds.

vulnerabilities.title: Last-Hour Voting Restriction Bypass via deposit_for Triggering Poke for Any Voted Token
vulnerabilities.summary: VoterV3.vote() restricts voting in the last hour of an epoch (epochVoteEnd check) for non-whitelisted, non-AVM tokens. However, VoterV3.poke() lacks this restriction — it only checks epochVoteStart. VotingEscrow.deposit_for() has no authentication requirement and triggers poke() for any voted token. Since msg.sender for poke() is the VE contract (passes `msg.sender == _ve` check), anyone can force a vote weight recalculation during the restricted last hour by depositing 1 wei into any voted toke

description.desc: poke() only checks `_timestamp <= epochVoteStart` (line 139), NOT epochVoteEnd; contrast vote() which checks `_timestamp >= epochVoteEnd` (line 161)
Affected function: deposit_for

vulnerabilities.impact: The epochVoteEnd restriction was designed to prevent last-minute vote manipulation (vote sniping). This bypass renders it ineffective for any voted token. Impact includes: (1) Gauge emission manipulation — attacker can shift emissions to preferred pools in the last minutes. (2) Bribe extraction — by inflating vote weight for a pool with bribes, attacker captures disproportionate bribe rewards. (3) Unfair advantage — competitors cannot respond since vote() is restricted. The severity is amplified by the short epoch duration (WEEK=1800s), making the attack window a significant fraction of the epoch.

vulnerabilities.title: RewardsDistributor Permanent Token Loss from checkpoint_token 20-Iteration Cap with WEEK=1800
vulnerabilities.summary: RewardsDistributor._checkpoint_token() distributes incoming tokens across weekly periods using a loop capped at 20 iterations. With WEEK=1800 seconds, 20 iterations covers only 36,000 seconds (10 hours). If checkpoint_token is not called for >10 hours, the loop exits without distributing tokens to the missed periods. The undistributed tokens remain in the contract but are never assigned to any tokens_per_week slot, making them permanently unclaimable by users.

description.desc: for (uint i = 0; i < 20; i++) { next_week = this_week + WEEK; ... } — if 20 iterations exhausted before reaching block.timestamp, remaining to_distribute is unassigned

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL severity. With WEEK=1800 seconds, even moderate operational delays (>10 hours) permanently remove tokens from the distribution system. The lost tokens inflate token_last_balance relative to sum(tokens_per_week), creating a permanent solvency gap in the rewards system. Over time, if gaps occur repeatedly, significant rebase rewards could be lost. The only recovery is manual intervention by the owner using withdrawERC20.

vulnerabilities.title: Unbounded Loop in AutoVotingEscrowManager getTotalVotingPower
vulnerabilities.summary: The `getTotalVotingPower` function iterates over all AVM instances, and each instance iterates over all its locks (up to 1024). This nested loop will quickly exceed the block gas limit, causing the function to revert.

description.desc: Nested unbounded loops with heavy internal calls

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The affected function is view-only, so no state-changing operations are directly blocked. However: (1) Any on-chain contract that calls getTotalVotingPower() will revert, breaking composability. (2) Off-chain UIs and indexers relying on this function will fail. (3) The issue becomes worse over time as more users adopt auto-voting. (4) No mitigation exists without contract upgrade since there's no pagination parameter.

vulnerabilities.title: Bribe reward token set can be spammed with many connector tokens causing getReward to exceed gas limits and brick reward claiming
vulnerabilities.summary: Bribe.notifyRewardAmount() allows any caller to add a new rewards token if tokenHandler marks it as a connector (or it is already a bribe token). Each new token is pushed into bribeTokens. Later, gaugeManager-driven reward claiming may pass large token arrays (or iterate over bribeTokens off-chain), and each token triggers earned() which itself loops over epochs. A spammer can deposit dust amounts across many connector tokens to inflate bribeTokens length, making reward claiming prohibitively ex

description.desc: unbounded growth of reward token list via public function

vulnerabilities.impact: The confirmed code defect is the absence of safeguards on bribeTokens growth. Missing: (1) cap on array length, (2) minimum reward amount, (3) token removal mechanism, (4) pagination in earned(). The impact ranges from degraded API performance (veNFTAPI becomes unusable) to potential DoS on reward claiming if gaugeManager passes all bribeTokens. With WEEK=1800s, the earned() function's epoch loop compounds the issue significantly. Storage bloat from many zero-reward tokens is permanent.

vulnerabilities.title: TokenHandler blacklist emits no Blacklisted event due to early return, breaking monitoring and allowing stealth delisting
vulnerabilities.summary: TokenHandler._blacklist swaps and pops the token from whiteListed and then returns immediately, making the subsequent `emit Blacklisted(...)` unreachable. This prevents on-chain event-based monitoring from detecting delistings reliably and can break indexers or downstream systems that rely on events to track allowed tokens for gauge creation/rewards.

description.desc: unreachable event emission due to early return

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The state changes are correct (token is properly removed from whitelist), but the missing event breaks the event log integrity. Off-chain systems (indexers, dashboards, monitoring bots) that rely on Blacklisted/BlacklistConnector events will have stale or incorrect data about which tokens are whitelisted. This is a real code defect regardless of whether it can be profitably exploited. The same pattern affects both _blacklist() and blacklistConnector(), affecting two separate token management workflows.

vulnerabilities.title: Bribe `withdraw` Silently Fails Allowing Phantom Votes
vulnerabilities.summary: If a gauge's internal bribe contract is replaced, users can retain their bribe deposits in the old contract while their votes are cleared, allowing them to double-dip rewards.

description.desc: Missing revert on insufficient balance allows phantom votes
Affected function: _reset

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact is a state desynchronization between VoterV3 and Bribe contracts after bribe replacement. Users retain phantom deposits in old bribe contracts, violating INV-ECON-004 from the system perspective (total tracked bribe deposits across old+new contracts exceed the user's actual voting weight). If the old bribe contract continues to receive any rewards (e.g., from trailing fee distributions), the user can claim them while also earning from the new bribe. Even without double rewards, the totalSupply in the old bribe contract is inflated, diluting rewards for other users who haven't reset.

vulnerabilities.title: delegateBySig Replay Attack Due to Nonce Not Incremented by Direct delegate()
vulnerabilities.summary: The delegate() function does not increment the nonces mapping, so previously signed delegateBySig messages remain valid after a user calls delegate() directly. An attacker who received a valid delegation signature can submit it at any time to override the signatory's subsequent direct delegate() calls, redirecting all of the victim's veNFT governance voting power.

description.desc: delegate() calls _delegate() but never increments nonces[msg.sender]. delegateBySig at line 627-657 checks nonce == nonces[signatory]++. Old signatures remain valid after direct delegate() calls.

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact. The vulnerability allows an attacker to override a user's current delegation preference using a stale signature. This redirects all of the victim's veNFT governance voting power (used for IVotes/IBlackHoleVotes governance). The impact scales with the number of users who have both (a) signed delegateBySig messages and (b) subsequently changed delegation via delegate(). Given that delegateBySig is specifically designed for gasless delegation workflows (common in governance UIs), this pattern is realistic.

vulnerabilities.title: Expired Lock Merge into SMNFT Grants Free Bonus Voting Power
vulnerabilities.summary: The merge function does not check that the source token has an active lock for non-permanent sources. An expired non-permanent lock can be merged into an SMNFT target, burning the expired tokens and granting SMNFT bonus voting power (10% by default). The user effectively converts withdrawable tokens into permanent voting power with a free bonus, when the intent is that SMNFT conversions require commitment (non-expired tokens).

description.desc: Only _locked1 (destination) checked for expiration: require(_locked1.end > block.timestamp || _locked1.isPermanent). No corresponding check for _locked0 (source) when !_locked0.isPermanent.

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect allows users to convert expired (withdrawable) tokens into SMNFT voting power with a 10% bonus. While the tokens ARE burned (so there is a real cost), the missing check creates an inconsistency with other conversion paths and grants bonus voting power without the intended time-lock commitment. Over time, this could lead to: (1) gradual inflation of SMNFT voting power as users intentionally let locks expire before merging, (2) dilution of other veNFT holders' voting power, (3) distortion of gauge voting and bribe incentives. The economic impact scales with the number of users exploiting this path and the total amount of expired tokens merged.

vulnerabilities.title: lockPermanent Missing Reentrancy Guard Enables Callback Exploitation via Voter.poke
vulnerabilities.summary: lockPermanent and unlockPermanent lack the nonreentrant modifier. lockPermanent calls IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId) when voted[_tokenId] is true, which makes external calls to Bribe contracts (deposit/withdraw). If any of these external calls have a callback mechanism (e.g., via token hooks or contract receiver), state could be manipulated between the permanentLockBalance update and the checkpoint/storage write.

description.desc: lockPermanent modifies permanentLockBalance and locked[_tokenId], calls _checkpoint, stores locked[_tokenId], then calls external IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId) - all without nonreentrant modifier

vulnerabilities.impact: The missing nonreentrant modifier is a genuine code defect that breaks the contract's defensive pattern. All 8 other state-modifying functions use nonreentrant, but lockPermanent and unlockPermanent do not. While current exploitation paths through Bribe callbacks are infeasible, the GaugeManager calls represent unverifiable external call surfaces. If GaugeManager or its downstream contracts are ever modified to include callbacks (or if a custom GaugeManager implementation provides them), the missing guard could become exploitable. The impact of potential exploitation would be corruption of permanentLockBalance and totalSupply calculations affecting rebase distribution and governance.

vulnerabilities.title: disableAutoVoting Permanent Lockout When AVM Has Already Voted in Current Epoch
vulnerabilities.summary: disableAutoVoting calls IVoter(voter).reset(tokenId) which has the onlyNewEpoch modifier. If the AVM has already voted for this tokenId in the current epoch, lastVoted[tokenId] >= epochStart(now), causing reset to revert with 'VOTED'. Additionally, the voted flag prevents transferFrom. Users cannot exit AVM until the next epoch, and with WEEK=1800, the next epoch window is the 1200-second non-last-hour window of the next 30-minute epoch.

description.desc: disableAutoVoting calls IVoter(voter).reset(tokenId) which has onlyNewEpoch modifier checking lastVoted[tokenId]. AVM sets lastVoted when voting via voteLocks -> voter.vote.

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. All AVM-enabled veNFT holders are affected every epoch after the AVM executor votes. Users temporarily lose the ability to exit AVM and regain control of their veNFT position. With WEEK=1800, the lockout window is at most ~600 seconds (10 minutes). With a more standard WEEK value (7 days), the lockout could extend up to almost a full week, making this significantly more impactful. The defect prevents emergency exits during volatile market conditions when users may urgently need to change their voting strategy or transfer their veNFT.

vulnerabilities.title: AutoVotingEscrow.voteLocks Claims Rebases for All Managed Tokens Without Access Control on claim()
vulnerabilities.summary: AutoVotingEscrow.voteLocks calls RewardsDistributor.claim(tokenId) for every active lock before voting. RewardsDistributor.claim() has no access control - anyone can call it for any tokenId. For active locks, claim() compounds rewards via deposit_for. This means the AVM executor determines WHEN rebases are compounded for all AVM-managed tokens, and any external caller can also force-compound. This could be weaponized to force-compound rebases at unfavorable times or to trigger poke side effects.

description.desc: voteLocks calls IRewardsDistributor(rewardsDistributor).claim(tokenId) before IVoter(voter).vote(). claim() has no access control and triggers deposit_for which triggers poke

vulnerabilities.impact: No impact - the described behavior is correct and intended. Anyone force-compounding a rebase for another user only benefits that user. The bribe redistribution is the mathematically correct outcome of the balance increase.
